what_it_does: A scenario library that walks through the 'Live Intent Resolution Surface' pipeline: it shows how vague, half-formed user inputs are progressively refined across seven diverse domains into concrete, buildable artifact packages, using anchors, tags, possibility cards, schema activation, missing-information maps, decision ledgers, visible assumptions, downstream workflows, and retained memory.
observations: Seven end-to-end scenarios cover software, education, policy rollout, art installation, household routine, data advocacy, and accessibility planning.

Each scenario follows a repeated temporal pattern: half-thought → anchors → possibility cards → reactions → tags → artifact candidates → schemas → UI surfaces → missing map → decisions → handoff → downstream build → memory.
The system uses incrementing tags, a decision ledger with Decision/Reason/Effect, and a visible missing-information map.
Possibility cards are generated first rather than jumping to a single solution, avoiding premature collapse.
Assumptions are surfaced and can be accepted, edited, or rejected by the user.
An 'autopilot with visible assumptions' mode lets the system proceed without blocking.
Downstream workflows are shown for design, code, test, deployment, writing, and media generation.
Memory is retained at both user level and project level for future sessions.
No actual code or executable schema definitions appear; the file is narrative and diagrammatic.
standouts: The explicit, repeated temporal resolution pattern across every scenario.
The decision ledger plus visible assumptions as a core interaction mechanism.
The breadth of artifact types, from web apps to lesson packs to physical household systems.
Memory retention framed as a first-class output of each scenario.
conventions: Markdown headings organize scenarios into T0, T1, T2 ... Tn phases.
Text diagrams and indented lists represent branching artifact maps, schemas, and handoff packages.
Tags are written as snake_case labels with +1 increments.
Decision ledger entries consistently include Decision, Reason, and Effect.
Missing-information maps list unresolved fields before generating targeted surfaces.
Each scenario ends with memory retained for the user and project.
Scenario 7 breaks the pattern by ending abruptly without a final package or memory section.
concerns: Scenario 7 is truncated mid-sentence, leaving the example incomplete.
Schemas are named but never defined, so their contents are unclear from this file alone.
Tags and decision ledgers are illustrative prose, not a machine-readable format.
Some assumptions are accepted by default with only a visible label, which could hide defaults.
The scenarios are long and repetitive, which may make maintenance difficult.
There is no explicit schema for how user reactions map to tag increments.
notes: A design-narrative document that turns an abstract intent-resolution pipeline into concrete, walkthrough scenarios across wildly different problem domains.
questions: What is the exact runtime structure of the 'fan-out/fan-in' worker architecture mentioned in Scenario 1?
Where are the schemas defined that are activated by name in each scenario?
How are tags, decision ledgers, and memory actually persisted and retrieved across sessions?
What triggers the transition from conversation to downstream build workflows?
Is this file intended as a specification, a design fiction, or documentation for an existing system?
Why is Scenario 7 truncated, and is the missing content available elsewhere?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: the stated purpose is to show the system's temporal behavior across examples, and the file does exactly that through seven end-to-end scenarios.
apparent_themes: Progressive refinement of vague intent into buildable artifacts.
Co-design with the user through generated surfaces and reactions.
Visible assumptions and user control over defaults.
Multi-artifact packages rather than single deliverables.
intention_signals: To model a co-design conversation where the system helps a user move from vague discomfort to a structured, executable artifact package without forcing a premature solution.
novelty: It treats intent resolution as an explicit, time-ordered, multi-surface dialogue with visible assumptions and memory, and applies the same pattern to both software and non-software artifacts.
contribution: A reusable scenario template and a diverse set of worked examples that make the intent-resolution pipeline concrete for implementers and reviewers.
summary_for_embedding: A Markdown scenario library illustrating the 'Live Intent Resolution Surface' pipeline across seven domains, showing how half-thought user inputs are refined into buildable artifact packages via anchors, schemas, decision ledgers, and memory.
intention_for_embedding: To provide concrete, end-to-end walkthroughs of a progressive intent-resolution and co-design system for use in building or explaining the surface.
